title: Creating threads
instruction:
Two ways to create a thread:

1. Extend `Thread` and override `run()`
2. Pass a `Runnable` to `Thread` — preferred

Call `.start()` (not `.run()` — start() creates a new thread; run() executes in the current thread).
successMessage: Threads run concurrently — output order varies. .join() waits for a thread to finish. Thread.sleep(ms) pauses the thread. Always interrupt-handle sleep: catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }

title: Race conditions — the silent enemy
instruction:
When multiple threads modify shared data without synchronisation, you get incorrect results:

```java
counter++;  // NOT atomic! Three operations: read, add, write
```

This is a race condition. Fix it with `synchronized` or `AtomicInteger`.
successMessage: unsafeCounter is almost never 10000 — threads overwrite each other's work. AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet() is a single atomic operation. For counters and flags: use AtomicInteger, AtomicBoolean.

title: synchronized — mutex lock
successMessage: Total is always 2000 — money is conserved. Without synchronized, threads could read the same balance simultaneously and both subtract, losing money. Synchronized prevents this.

title: ExecutorService — thread pools
successMessage: Pool of 4 threads processes 8 tasks. Future.get() blocks until the result is ready. Results come back in submission order — even though execution was concurrent.

title: CompletableFuture — async pipelines
instruction:
`CompletableFuture` is the modern async API — chain operations without blocking:

```java
CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fetchData())
    .thenApply(data -> processData(data))
    .thenAccept(result -> saveResult(result));
```

Each step runs when the previous one completes.
successMessage: supplyAsync → thenApply → thenApply is a pipeline. thenCombine merges two independent futures. This is how modern Java web frameworks handle concurrent requests.

title: Build a parallel word counter
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Write a parallel word frequency counter:
1. Create a list of 10 text strings
2. Use `ExecutorService` with 4 threads to process each string
3. Each thread counts words in its string and returns a `Map<String, Integer>`
4. Merge all maps in the main thread
5. Print the top 5 most frequent words

Hint: use `Callable<Map<String, Integer>>` and `Future`.
hint: pool.submit(new Callable<Map<String,Integer>>() { public Map<String,Integer> call() { ... } }) or lambda with explicit type.

codeChecks.message: Use ExecutorService with a thread pool.
Use Callable/Future for tasks that return results.
Shut down the pool when done.
Use a Map for word frequencies.

successMessage: Parallel word counting with thread pooling. Real search engines use exactly this pattern — divide the corpus, count in parallel, merge results. Chapter 20 done!
